Original Conclusion (Agent1):

Based on the retrieved evidence, there is no explicit mention of the proximal wall convergence or divergence in Class II cavities on deciduous molars. While some references discuss general tooth contours and enamel thickness, none specify directional trends (convergence/divergence) of proximal walls in Class II preparations. Therefore, claims about anatomical convergence lack support in available sources.
\boxed{\text{A}}
Even though the evidence does not explicitly confirm convergence, it supports the fundamental principle that enamel rods align along the long axis of the tooth—an established fact in dental anatomy—and thus, proximal walls would naturally follow this orientation rather than converge toward the occlusal surface. After analyzing the retrieved evidence:
There is no direct information confirming that proximal walls of a Class II cavity on a deciduous molar converge toward the occlusal surface. However, enamel rods in deciduous teeth typically orient parallel to the long axis of the tooth, meaning that proximal walls should follow this direction—not necessarily converge toward the occlusal surface. Thus, Option A remains supported by basic dental anatomy principles, whereas Option B lacks evidentiary backing.
